name: अक्षरब्रह्मयोग
teaching: What you dwell on, you become — and what fills the mind at the end of life reveals what filled it all along. Krishna speaks plainly about death: the cosmos itself breathes in and out, worlds arise and dissolve, and beyond all of it stands the imperishable, reached by remembrance. Therefore remember now; the final thought is only the sum of every ordinary one. What is held in the mind across a life is what the mind holds at the end — so remembrance is made a daily art. Two paths lead out of the world; the chapter teaches the bright one.
questions: If your last thought will be your most practiced thought, what are you practicing?
What in your life belongs to the cycle that returns, and what to that which does not?
Could you pair one daily act — breath, walking, work — with remembrance, starting today?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
किं तद् ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम ।
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
kiṃ tad brahma kimadhyātmaṃ kiṃ karma puruṣottama
adhibhūtaṃ ca kiṃ proktamadhidaivaṃ kimucyate
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: what is that Brahman? What is the adhyatma? What is karma, Purushottama? What is called adhibhuta, and what adhidaiva?
verses.translation: Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

anvaya.meaning: what is that Brahman, what the adhyatma
what is karma, O Purushottama
and what is declared the adhibhuta
what is called the adhidaiva

verses.explanation: Arjuna pounces on the loose threads of 7.29–30 and asks five definitions in two lines — a student's rapid-fire after a teacher's deliberate cliffhanger. The questions look academic; the last one (next verse) will reveal they are anything but.
verses.connection: Directly picks up the undefined terms Krishna dangled at chapter 7's close.
verses.reflection: Keep Arjuna's habit today: when big words blur — Brahman, karma, adhyatma — stop and ask for definitions.
verses.background: Arjuna, hearing the dense final words of chapter seven, asks for definitions — seven rapid questions: what is Brahman, adhyatma, karma, adhibhuta, adhidaiva, adhiyajna — and the one beneath them all: how are You known at the time of death by the self-controlled?

verses.devanagari:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहेऽस्मिन्मधुसूदन ।
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः॥
verses.transliteration:
adhiyajñaḥ kathaṃ ko'tra dehe'sminmadhusūdana
prayāṇakāle ca kathaṃ jñeyo'si niyatātmabhiḥ
verses.essence: How is the adhiyajna here in this body, Madhusudana? And how, at the time of departure, are you to be known by the self-controlled?
verses.translation: Who and how is Adhiyajna here in this body, O destroyer of Madhu (Krishna)? And how at the time of death, art Thou to be known by the self-controlled?

anvaya.meaning: the adhiyajna — how, who, here
in this body, O Madhusudana
and at the time of departure, how
are you to be known by the self-controlled

verses.explanation: The seventh question is the live one: prayāṇa-kāle — at the hour of going. Beneath the terminology, Arjuna is asking what every mortal eventually asks: how does one hold the highest knowledge at the moment everything is being taken away? The whole chapter is the answer to this last question.
verses.connection: Completes the seven questions; the final clause sets the chapter's true subject — dying well.
verses.reflection: Ask the practical question under all theology once today: how is He known at the hour it matters most?
verses.background: Arjuna, hearing the dense final words of chapter seven, asks for definitions — seven rapid questions: what is Brahman, adhyatma, karma, adhibhuta, adhidaiva, adhiyajna — and the one beneath them all: how are You known at the time of death by the self-controlled?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमं स्वभावोऽध्यात्ममुच्यते ।
भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञितः॥
verses.transliteration:
akṣaraṃ brahma paramaṃ svabhāvo'dhyātmamucyate
bhūtabhāvodbhavakaro visargaḥ karmasaṃjñitaḥ
verses.essence: The Imperishable is the supreme Brahman; its presence in each being is the adhyatma; the creative offering that gives rise to beings is called karma.
verses.translation: Brahman is the Imperishable, the Supreme; Its essential nature is called Self-knowledge; the offering (to the gods) which causes existence and manifestation of beings and which also sustains them is called action.

anvaya.meaning: the Imperishable is the supreme Brahman
its own-being (in each) is called the adhyatma
that which brings about the arising of beings
the creative emission is named karma

verses.explanation: Three answers in one breath, each a doorway: Brahman is the akṣara, what cannot be eroded; adhyātma is that same reality as it lives in *you*, your own deepest nature; and karma, in its widest sense, is visarga — the sacrificial pouring-forth by which existence keeps generating existence. Definitions like compressed scriptures.
verses.connection: Answers the first three of 8.1's questions with three strokes.
verses.schools_note: Arjuna's seven questions get compressed answers, and the schools unpack 'akshara' differently: Shankara — the supreme immutable Brahman; Ramanuja — also the freed soul and the syllable OM by context across the chapter. 'Karma' defined as visarga — the creative offering-forth — is read by all as the Gita re-rooting ritual vocabulary in cosmology: action as the universe's own liturgy.
verses.reflection: Define your terms this morning: the imperishable in you, your inner nature, and the offering you call work.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम् ।
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर॥
verses.transliteration:
adhibhūtaṃ kṣaro bhāvaḥ puruṣaścādhidaivatam
adhiyajño'hamevātra dehe dehabhṛtāṃ vara
verses.essence: The adhibhuta is perishable existence; the purusha is the adhidaiva; and I myself am the adhiyajna here in the body, best of the embodied.
verses.translation: Adhibhuta (knowledge of the elements) pertains to My perishable Nature and the Purusha or the Soul is the Adhidaiva; I alone am the Adhiyajna here in this body, O best among the embodied (men).

anvaya.meaning: the adhibhuta is the perishable mode
and the purusha is the adhidaiva
the adhiyajna am I myself, here
in the body, O best of body-bearers

verses.explanation: The remaining answers, ending in the chapter's quiet thunderclap: adhiyajño 'ham evātra dehe — the lord of sacrifice is *I, here, in this body*. The receiving altar of every offering a person ever makes is not in a temple or a heaven; it is resident within the offerer. All the chapter's death-instruction follows from this nearness.
verses.connection: Completes the definitions and plants the key: the One to be remembered at death already dwells inside.
verses.reflection: See today's world as the perishable basis, and yourself as the witness it plays before.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
अन्तकाले च मामेव स्मरन्मुक्त्वा कलेवरम् ।
यः प्रयाति स मद्भावं याति नास्त्यत्र संशयः॥
verses.transliteration:
antakāle ca māmeva smaranmuktvā kalevaram
yaḥ prayāti sa madbhāvaṃ yāti nāstyatra saṃśayaḥ
verses.essence: And whoever, at the end, leaves the body remembering me alone, goes to my being — of this there is no doubt.
verses.translation: And whosoever, leaving the body, goes forth remembering Me alone, at the time of death, he attains My Being: there is no doubt about this.

anvaya.meaning: and at the end-time, me alone
remembering, releasing the body
who departs, that one to my being
goes — there is no doubt herein

verses.explanation: The central promise, flat and unhedged: final remembrance of him delivers the rememberer into his being, nāsty atra saṁśayaḥ. The last act of attention functions as the soul's forwarding address. Everything that follows — the practice, the syllable, the timing — exists to make this remembrance possible when it matters most. Anta-kale cha mam eva — the death-remembrance verse on which whole devotional cultures were built: the name on the lips at the end, the Gita read at the bedside, the practice of japa justified in one line. Commentators add the realism of the next verse immediately: the final thought is not performed at the end but rehearsed across a lifetime — the deathbed merely publishes the habit.
verses.connection: Answers 8.2's death-question directly; the rest of the chapter is its method and fine print.
verses.reflection: Practice the final thought in advance: end one ordinary day remembering Him, as rehearsal.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
यं यं वापि स्मरन्भावं त्यजत्यन्ते कलेवरम् ।
तं तमेवैति कौन्तेय सदा तद्भावभावितः॥
verses.transliteration:
yaṃ yaṃ vāpi smaranbhāvaṃ tyajatyante kalevaram
taṃ tamevaiti kaunteya sadā tadbhāvabhāvitaḥ
verses.essence: Whatever state of being one remembers when leaving the body at the end, Kaunteya, that very state one attains, ever shaped by that absorption.
verses.translation: Whosoever at the end leaves the body, thinking of any being, to that being only does he go, O son of Kunti (Arjuna), because of his constant thought of that being.

anvaya.word: यं यं वा अपि स्मरन् भावं
anvaya.meaning: whatever state remembering
one leaves the body at the end
that very (state) one reaches, O Kaunteya
ever made-of-that-state by absorption

verses.explanation: The general law beneath 8.5's special case: the final thought is a vector, and the soul travels along it. But the verse closes its own loophole before anyone games it — sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ: the dying thought is not chosen at the deathbed; it is the residue of a lifetime's dwelling. One cannot cram for this examination; the last thought is simply the loudest habit. Yam yam vapi smaran bhavam — whatever state one remembers at the end, to that one goes, sada tad-bhava-bhavitah — being ever steeped in it. The tradition's gloss is the famous one: the mind at death is the average of the mind in life. The doctrine birthed the practice of constant remembrance precisely because it abolished last-minute religion.
verses.connection: Generalizes 8.5 into law — and explains why the next verse must say 'at all times'.
verses.schools_note: The famous 'final-thought' doctrine. The tradition reads it through the story of Bharata (reborn a deer for dying absorbed in one); commentators across schools agree on its practical edge: since death's hour is unschedulable, the only strategy is to make remembrance constant — which 8.7 immediately commands.
verses.reflection: Mind what you marinate in — whatever state you feed daily is the state that will claim the exit.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च ।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्मामेवैष्यस्यसंशयः (orसंशयम्)॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmātsarveṣu kāleṣu māmanusmara yudhya ca
mayyarpitamanobuddhirmāmevaiṣyasyasaṃśayaḥ
verses.essence: Therefore at all times remember me, and fight. With mind and understanding surrendered to me, you will come to me — beyond doubt.
verses.translation: Therefore at all times remember Me only and fight. With mind and intellect fixed (or absorbed) in Me, thou shalt doubtessly come to Me alone.

anvaya.meaning: therefore, at all times
remember me — and fight
with mind and buddhi offered into me
to me alone you will come, doubtlessly

verses.explanation: The Gita's whole reconciliation in four words: mām anusmara yudhya ca — remember me, *and fight*. Not remembrance instead of the battle, nor the battle postponing remembrance: both at once, inner gaze fixed while the hands do their fierce duty. The verse is the charter of every contemplative-in-action since. Tasmat sarveshu kaleshu mam anusmara yudhya cha — therefore at all times remember Me, and fight: the Gita's whole method in five words. The commentaries treasure the conjunction: not remember instead of fighting, nor fight then remember — both, simultaneously, the inner thread unbroken through the outer act. Mayy arpita-mano-buddhih: mind and intellect both deposited, hands fully at war.
verses.connection: Converts 8.6's law into Arjuna's standing orders: constant remembrance, undiminished engagement.
verses.reflection: Carry the full instruction into one task today: remember — and fight. Both, together, all day.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा नान्यगामिना ।
परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन्॥
verses.transliteration:
orsaṃśayama abhyāsayogayuktena cetasā nānyagāminā
paramaṃ puruṣaṃ divyaṃ yāti pārthānucintayan
verses.essence: With a mind harnessed by the yoga of practice, straying to nothing else, meditating — one goes to the supreme divine Purusha, Partha.
verses.translation: With the mind not moving towards any other thing, made steadfast by the method of habitual meditation, and constantly meditating, one goes to the Supreme Person, the Resplendent, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: yoked by the yoga of practice
with a mind not going elsewhere
to the supreme divine Purusha
one goes, O Partha, continuously meditating

verses.explanation: How 'at all times' becomes feasible: abhyāsa-yoga, the repetition that wears remembrance into a groove until the mind, left alone, flows there by default — nānya-gāminā, not wandering off. Anucintayan: thinking-along, a current of recollection beneath the day's traffic. Constancy is not strain; it is a trained riverbed.
verses.connection: Supplies the training method behind 8.7's command, echoing 6.35's abhyasa.
verses.reflection: Give attention one steady channel today; consciousness flows where practice has dug.
verses.background: Krishna answers each term in a breath and settles on the question that matters: whoever leaves the body remembering Me alone attains My being, for one becomes what one constantly dwells on — therefore remember Me at all times, and fight, mind and intellect set on Me.

verses.devanagari:
कविं पुराणमनुशासितार-
मणोरणीयंसमनुस्मरेद्यः ।
सर्वस्य धातारमचिन्त्यरूप-
मादित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात्॥
verses.transliteration:
kaviṃ purāṇamanuśāsitāraṃ aṇoraṇīyaṃsamanusmaredyaḥ
sarvasya dhātāramacintyarūpaṃ ādityavarṇaṃ tamasaḥ parastāt
verses.essence: The seer, the ancient, the ruler, subtler than the subtle, supporter of all, of inconceivable form, sun-colored beyond the darkness —
verses.translation: Whosoever meditates on the Omniscient, the Ancient, the Ruler (of the whole world), minuter than an atom, the supporter of all, of inconceivable form, effulgent like the sun and beyond the darkness of ignorance.

anvaya.meaning: the seer, the ancient, the ruler
subtler than the atom — who remembers (him)
supporter of all, of unthinkable form
sun-colored, beyond the darkness

verses.explanation: What, concretely, to remember: eight facets given as handholds for meditation — the poet-seer who conceived all this, older than time, smaller than the smallest, holding everything, shaped beyond imagining, and shining āditya-varṇam, sun-colored, on the far side of all darkness. A portrait built to be gazed at, not merely parsed.
verses.connection: Begins the deathbed meditation proper (8.9–13): the object described before the technique.
verses.reflection: Meditate once on the Ancient Seer — subtler than subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness — for one minute.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
प्रयाणकाले मनसाऽचलेन
भक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव ।
भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक्
स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम्॥
verses.transliteration:
prayāṇakāle manasā'calena bhaktyā yukto yogabalena caiva
bhruvormadhye prāṇamāveśya samyak sa taṃ paraṃ puruṣamupaiti divyam
verses.essence: At the time of departure, with unmoving mind, with devotion and the strength of yoga, fixing the life-breath between the brows — one reaches that supreme divine Purusha.
verses.translation: At the time of death, with unshaken mind, endowed with devotio, by the power of Yoga, fixing the whole life-breath in the middle of the two eyrows, he reaches that resplendent Supreme Person.

anvaya.meaning: at departure-time, with unmoving mind
joined with devotion, and with yoga's strength
placing the prana rightly between the brows

anvaya.word: सः तं परं पुरुषम् उपैति दिव्यम्
anvaya.meaning: he attains that supreme divine Purusha

verses.explanation: The technique at the threshold: mind stilled, heart given, and the life-current gathered upward to the brow-center — the body's own exit ritual. Note the trinity required: bhakti supplies the direction, yoga-bala the strength, acala manas the stillness. Dying is treated here as the final āsana, performable with skill.
verses.connection: Pairs with 8.9: the gaze's object, now the gaze's physical method at the last hour.
verses.reflection: Gather your life-force gently between the brows for a few breaths tonight, with devotion, and rest there.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
यदक्षरं वेदविदो वदन्ति
विशन्ति यद्यतयो वीतरागाः ।
यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति
तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण प्रवक्ष्ये॥
verses.transliteration:
yadakṣaraṃ vedavido vadanti viśanti yadyatayo vītarāgāḥ
yadicchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti tatte padaṃ saṃgraheṇa pravakṣye
verses.essence: That which the Veda-knowers call the Imperishable, which the passion-free ascetics enter, desiring which they live in continence — that state I shall declare to you in brief.
verses.translation: That which is declared Imperishable by those who know the Vedas, that which the self-controlled (ascetics or Sannyasins) and passion-free enter, that desiring which celibacy is practised that goal I will declare to thee in brief.

anvaya.meaning: what the Veda-knowers call the Imperishable
which the strivers free of passion enter
desiring which they practice brahmacharya
that goal I shall declare to you in summary

verses.explanation: Credentials before the secret: the goal about to be taught is the same one the Upanishadic seers named, the same one renunciates spend lifetimes of austerity approaching. Krishna offers it saṅgraheṇa — in summary — to a man with hours, not decades: the battlefield condensation of the forest's longest curriculum.
verses.connection: Frames 8.12–13 as the distilled essence of the entire ascetic tradition.
verses.reflection: Honor the syllable the renunciates die into — let OM open or close your practice today.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च ।
मूध्न्यार्धायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvadvārāṇi saṃyamya mano hṛdi nirudhya ca
mūdhnyā^^rdhāyātmanaḥ prāṇamāsthito yogadhāraṇām
verses.essence: Closing all the body's gates, confining the mind in the heart, drawing the life-breath into the head, established in yogic concentration —
verses.translation: Having closed all the gates, confined the mind in the heart and fixed the life-breath in the head, engaged in the practice of concentration.

anvaya.meaning: restraining all the gates
and confining the mind in the heart
placing one's prana in the head
established in yogic concentration

verses.explanation: The nine-gated city of 5.13 returns for its final scene: gates closed not in defeat but in ceremony, the mind brought home to the heart, the life-force walked up its inner staircase to the crown. The body, which spent a lifetime opening outward, is folded inward once — deliberately, like a letter being sealed.
verses.connection: The physical liturgy of departure, completing 8.10's brow-center instruction.
verses.reflection: Close the gates briefly today: eyes soft, voice still, heart gathered — the city quieted for its king.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
ओमित्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन् ।
यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
omityekākṣaraṃ brahma vyāharanmāmanusmaran
yaḥ prayāti tyajandehaṃ sa yāti paramāṃ gatim
verses.essence: Uttering Om, the one-syllabled Brahman, and remembering me — whoever departs thus, leaving the body, goes to the supreme goal.
verses.translation: Uttering the one-syllabled Om the Brahman and remembering Me, he who departs, leaving the body, attains to the Supreme Goal.

anvaya.meaning: 'Om' — the one-syllabled Brahman
uttering, remembering me
who departs, leaving the body
that one goes to the supreme goal

verses.explanation: The last breath given its word and its thought: Om on the lips — the syllable that is sound's seed and the Veda's whole compressed — and Krishna in the mind. Note the pairing: the impersonal vibration *and* the personal remembrance, together; the Gita declines to choose between them even at the very end.
verses.connection: Completes the death-yoga of 8.9–12: posture, breath, syllable, and the Name — the full send-off.
verses.reflection: Sound OM once slowly, remembering what it names — and notice the room it makes.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः ।
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः॥
verses.transliteration:
ananyacetāḥ satataṃ yo māṃ smarati nityaśaḥ
tasyāhaṃ sulabhaḥ pārtha nityayuktasya yoginaḥ
verses.essence: For the one who remembers me constantly with undivided mind — for that ever-harnessed yogi, Partha, I am easy to reach.
verses.translation: I am easily attainable by that ever-steadfast Yogi who constantly and daily remembers Me (for a long time), not thinking of anything else (with a single mind or one-pointed mind), O Partha (Arjuna).

anvaya.meaning: with mind on nothing else, constantly
who remembers me, perpetually
for that one I am easily attained, O Partha
for the ever-yoked yogi

verses.explanation: After the technical rigor of 8.10–13, a verse of pure relief: sulabhaḥ — *easy* to reach. The elaborate death-technique is the hard road for those who need it; for the one whose remembrance never stopped, no technique is required, because nothing was ever put down that must now be picked up. Love makes the examination open-book.
verses.connection: The merciful counterweight to the death-yoga: constancy of heart outranks virtuosity at the end.
verses.reflection: Be easy to reach today: think of Him constantly for one hour and test the promise of nearness.
verses.background: The death-meditation is detailed: the Person ancient, subtler than the subtle, sun-colored beyond darkness; the gates of the body closed, the breath drawn to the head, the syllable OM sounded with remembrance — and then the easier promise: for the ever-yoked who think of none else, I am easy to reach.

verses.devanagari:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम् ।
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः॥
verses.transliteration:
māmupetya punarjanma duḥkhālayamaśāśvatam
nāpnuvanti mahātmānaḥ saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ
verses.essence: Having come to me, the great souls do not take rebirth in this impermanent house of sorrow — they have reached the highest perfection.
verses.translation: Having attained Me these great souls do not again take birth (here) which is the place of pain and is non-eternal: they have reached the highest perfection (liberation).

anvaya.meaning: having attained me, rebirth
the abode of sorrow, the impermanent
they do not obtain — the great souls
having gone to the supreme perfection

verses.explanation: The destination's permanence, with the world given its bleakest honest epithets in passing: duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam — a lodging of sorrow, and a temporary one at that. Not a curse upon existence but a traveler's accurate review of the inn. Those who reach him do not check back in.
verses.connection: States what 8.14's easy arrival secures: the end of the round itself.
verses.reflection: Hold the destination clearly: reached once, the house of sorrow is not re-entered.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
आब्रह्मभुवनाल्लोकाः पुनरावर्तिनोऽर्जुन ।
मामुपेत्य तु कौन्तेय पुनर्जन्म न विद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
ābrahmabhuvanāllokāḥ punarāvartino'rjuna
māmupetya tu kaunteya punarjanma na vidyate
verses.essence: All worlds up to the realm of Brahma are subject to return, Arjuna. But having come to me, Kaunteya, there is no rebirth.
verses.translation: (All) the worlds including the world of Brahma are subject to return again, O Arjuna; but he who reaches Me, O son of Kunti, has no rirth.

anvaya.meaning: up to the world of Brahma, the worlds
are subject to return, O Arjuna
but having reached me, O Kaunteya
rebirth does not exist

verses.explanation: The cosmic ceiling drawn: even Brahma's own realm — the summit of every ladder austerity can climb — is on the circuit; its residents, however exalted, hold return tickets. One destination alone is exempt. The verse quietly devalues every heaven in advance: anything reachable by merit is rentable, not ownable.
verses.connection: Extends 8.15 upward through the entire cosmos: all stations revolve except the hub.
verses.reflection: Stop renovating rooms in burning buildings — even the highest worlds return; aim past them today.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तमहर्यद् ब्रह्मणो विदुः ।
रात्रिं युगसहस्रान्तां तेऽहोरात्रविदो जनाः॥
verses.transliteration:
sahasrayugaparyantamaharyad brahmaṇo viduḥ
rātriṃ yugasahasrāntāṃ te'horātravido janāḥ
verses.essence: Those who know Brahma's day to last a thousand ages, and his night a thousand ages — they are the knowers of day and night.
verses.translation: Those people who know the day of Brahma which is of a duration of a thousand Yugas (ages) and the night which is also of a thousand Yugas duration, they know day and night.

anvaya.meaning: extending to a thousand yugas
what they know as Brahma's day
and the night ending in a thousand yugas
they are the people who know day-and-night

verses.explanation: The scale of 8.16's wheel: one working day of the creator spans a thousand cosmic ages, his night the same — and our universes bloom and wither inside his office hours. Whoever has internalized *this* calendar, the verse says drily, actually knows what 'day and night' mean; everyone else is reading a child's clock.
verses.connection: Begins the cosmological zoom-out (8.17–19) that makes 8.16's 'return' visible at full scale.
verses.reflection: Zoom out once today: your busiest year is a flicker in Brahma's single day. Breathe accordingly.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
अव्यक्ताद् व्यक्तयः सर्वाः प्रभवन्त्यहरागमे ।
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके॥
verses.transliteration:
avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavantyaharāgame
rātryāgame pralīyante tatraivāvyaktasaṃjñake
verses.essence: At the coming of day, all manifest things stream forth from the unmanifest; at the coming of night they dissolve into that same unmanifest.
verses.translation: From the Unmanifested all the manifested (worlds) proceed at the coming of the 'day'; at the coming of the 'night' they dissolve verily into ï1thatï1 alone which is called the Unmanifested.

anvaya.meaning: from the unmanifest, all manifestations
stream forth at the coming of day
at the coming of night they dissolve
into that very one called the unmanifest

verses.explanation: The cosmic respiration: dawn of Brahma's day and the worlds exhale into form; his dusk, and they are inhaled back into the formless reservoir. Creation and dissolution lose their drama at this magnification — they are breathing, regular as tide, neither a beginning to celebrate nor an end to mourn.
verses.connection: The rhythm inside 8.17's calendar: manifestation as the universe's inhale and exhale.
verses.reflection: Watch one thing emerge this morning and dissolve this evening — the cosmic tide in miniature.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
भूतग्रामः स एवायं भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते ।
रात्र्यागमेऽवशः पार्थ प्रभवत्यहरागमे॥
verses.transliteration:
bhūtagrāmaḥ sa evāyaṃ bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate
rātryāgame'vaśaḥ pārtha prabhavatyaharāgame
verses.essence: This very same multitude of beings, arising again and again, dissolves helplessly at the coming of night, Partha, and streams forth at the coming of day.
verses.translation: This same multitude of beings, being born again and again, is dissolved, helplessly, O Arjuna (into the Unmanifested) at the coming of the night and comes forth at the coming of the day.

anvaya.meaning: this very same host of beings
having come to be, again and again, dissolves
at night's coming, helpless, O Partha
and comes forth at day's coming

verses.explanation: The verse's quiet horror is one word: avaśaḥ — *helplessly*. The same crowd of beings, bhūtvā bhūtvā, becoming and becoming, swept out and swept back with no vote in either direction. This is samsara seen from orbit: not a personal journey but a tide, and everyone unliberated is foam on it. The exit of 8.15 suddenly looks less like doctrine and more like rescue.
verses.connection: Personalizes 8.18's rhythm: the breathing cosmos, seen from inside, is compulsory recurrence.
verses.reflection: Be gentle with the helpless recurrences in your life — arising and dissolving is the universe's own habit.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः ।
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
parastasmāttu bhāvo'nyo'vyakto'vyaktātsanātanaḥ
yaḥ sa sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati
verses.essence: But beyond that unmanifest is another, eternal unmanifest existence — which does not perish when all beings perish.
verses.translation: But verily there exists, higher than this Unmanifested, another unmanifested Eternal, which is not destroyed when all beings are destroyed.

anvaya.meaning: but beyond that, another existence
unmanifest, (beyond) the unmanifest, eternal
which, when all beings
are perishing, does not perish

verses.explanation: Past the reservoir into which worlds dissolve stands a second unmanifest — avyakto 'vyaktāt — not the seed-state of things but what never entered the cycle at all. The first unmanifest is night between days; this one is what neither day nor night touch. When even the dissolution dissolves, this remains, undented.
verses.connection: The decisive move past 8.18–19's cycle: behind the breathing, something that does not breathe in and out.
verses.reflection: Lean once today on what does not perish when all manifestation perishes — it is nearer than the news.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
अव्यक्तोऽक्षर इत्युक्तस्तमाहुः परमां गतिम् ।
यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम॥
verses.transliteration:
avyakto'kṣara ityuktastamāhuḥ paramāṃ gatim
yaṃ prāpya na nivartante taddhāma paramaṃ mama
verses.essence: That unmanifest, called the Imperishable, they declare the supreme goal — attaining which, none return. That is my highest abode.
verses.translation: What is called the Unmanifested and the Imperishable, That they say is the highest goal. They who reach It do not return (to this Samsara). That is My highest abode (place or state).

anvaya.meaning: 'unmanifest', 'imperishable' — thus called
that they declare the supreme goal
attaining which they do not return
that is my supreme abode

verses.explanation: The chapter's threads tie: the akṣara of Arjuna's first question (8.1), the unfalling state of 8.20, and now a personal possessive that changes everything — dhāma paramaṁ *mama*. The Beyond-the-beyond is not a vacancy; it is his home address. The Absolute has a host.
verses.connection: Names 8.20's deathless ground — and claims it: the impersonal summit is his residence.
verses.reflection: Note where your roads lead: most loop back; one does not. Take three steps on the no-return road today.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
पुरुषः स परः पार्थ भक्त्या लभ्यस्त्वनन्यया ।
यस्यान्तःस्थानि भूतानि येन सर्वमिदं ततम्॥
verses.transliteration:
puruṣaḥ sa paraḥ pārtha bhaktyā labhyastvananyayā
yasyāntaḥsthāni bhūtāni yena sarvamidaṃ tatam
verses.essence: That supreme Purusha, Partha, within whom all beings dwell and by whom all this is pervaded, is attainable by undivided devotion.
verses.translation: That highest Purusha, O Arjuna, is attainable by unswerving devotion to Him alone within Whom all beings dwell and by Whom all this is pervaded.

anvaya.meaning: that Purusha, the supreme, O Partha
is attainable by devotion that is undivided
within whom beings dwell
by whom all this is pervaded

verses.explanation: How the unreachable is reached: bhaktyā tv ananyayā — by love with no second object. Not by the cosmological knowledge just displayed, not by austerity that scales only to Brahma's revolving realm: devotion alone crosses into the abode. The chapter that began with seven philosophical definitions answers, in the end, with one relationship.
verses.connection: Gives 8.21's abode its single key — anticipating 11.54's identical claim at the vision's end.
verses.reflection: Try the stated price of the Supreme today: devotion without a second object — bhaktya labhyas tv ananyaya.
verses.background: The teaching zooms out to cosmic scale: worlds up to Brahma's realm return again; a thousand-age day and night of Brahma breathe all beings out and in — but beyond the unmanifest stands another Unmanifest that never perishes, My supreme abode, attained by undivided devotion.

verses.devanagari:
यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः ।
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ॥
verses.transliteration:
yatra kāle tvanāvṛttimāvṛttiṃ caiva yoginaḥ
prayātā yānti taṃ kālaṃ vakṣyāmi bharatarṣabha
verses.essence: Now I shall tell you, Bharatarshabha, the times at which yogis departing return — and do not return.
verses.translation: Now I will tell thee, O chief of Bharatas, the times departing at which the Yogis will return or not return.

anvaya.meaning: at which time, non-return
and return, the yogis
departing, attain — that time
I shall declare, O bull of the Bharatas

verses.explanation: A final technical appendix is announced: the traditional lore of departure-times, the cosmic schedule by which the dying are said to be routed. Krishna includes the old map — Arjuna's world knew and trusted it — before quietly relativizing it at the section's end.
verses.connection: Opens the two-paths teaching (8.24–26), inherited from the Upanishads.
verses.reflection: Live one day so the hour of departure could fall anywhere in it without finding you unready.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.

verses.devanagari:
अग्निर्जोतिरहः शुक्लः षण्मासा उत्तरायणम् ।
तत्र प्रयाता गच्छन्ति ब्रह्म ब्रह्मविदो जनाः॥
verses.transliteration:
agnirjotirahaḥ śuklaḥ ṣaṇmāsā uttarāyaṇam
tatra prayātā gacchanti brahma brahmavido janāḥ
verses.essence: Fire, light, day, the bright fortnight, the six months of the sun's northern course — departing then, the knowers of Brahman go to Brahman.
verses.translation: Fire, light daytime, the bright fortnight, the six months of the northern path of the sun (the northern solstice) departing then (by these) men who know Brahman go to Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: fire, light, day, the bright (fortnight)
the six months of the northern course
departing then, they go
to Brahman — the Brahman-knowing people

verses.explanation: The luminous itinerary: every station on this route is a form of light — flame, brightness, day, waxing moon, the sun climbing north. Read literally, a calendar; read as the tradition's deeper voice does, a description of the *state* of departure: those who leave through light — through knowledge — do not come back.
verses.connection: The path of no return, mapped in the old solar vocabulary.
verses.schools_note: The bright and dark paths (8.24-26) divide interpreters: literalists map solstices and lunar fortnights of actual dying; Shankara and most classical readers take the times as presiding deities on the soul's two routes (devayana and pitriyana from the Upanishads); modern teachers often read them as states — clarity versus obscurity at death. All agree on 8.27's point: the yogi who knows both is bewildered by neither.
verses.reflection: Walk the bright path in miniature today: act in daylight clarity, openly, with nothing to hide.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.

verses.devanagari:
धूमो रात्रिस्तथा कृष्णः षण्मासा दक्षिणायनम् ।
तत्र चान्द्रमसं ज्योतिर्योगी प्राप्य निवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
dhūmo rātristathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇmāsā dakṣiṇāyanam
tatra cāndramasaṃ jyotiryogī prāpya nivartate
verses.essence: Smoke, night, the dark fortnight, the six months of the southern course — departing then, the yogi attains the lunar light and returns.
verses.translation: Attaining to the lunar light by smoke, night time, the dark fortnight also, the six months of the southern path of the sun (the southern solstice), the Yogi returns.

anvaya.meaning: smoke, night, likewise the dark (fortnight)
the six months of the southern course
there, the lunar light
the yogi, attaining, returns

verses.explanation: The mirrored route, every station dimmed: smoke for fire, night for day, waning for waxing, the sun's southern retreat. Its terminus is the moon's borrowed light — merit's heaven, lovely and finite — from which the traveler nivartate, comes back. Departure through dimness, through works without knowledge, circles home again.
verses.connection: The path of return: the same journey taken through shadow instead of light.
verses.reflection: Notice your smoke-path hours — the dim, half-asleep ones — and reclaim one of them for clarity.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.

verses.devanagari:
शुक्लकृष्णे गती ह्येते जगतः शाश्वते मते ।
एकया यात्यनावृत्तिमन्ययावर्तते पुनः॥
verses.transliteration:
śuklakṛṣṇe gatī hyete jagataḥ śāśvate mate
ekayā yātyanāvṛttimanyayāvartate punaḥ
verses.essence: These two paths of the world — the bright and the dark — are held to be eternal. By one, a person goes never to return; by the other, returns again.
verses.translation: The bright and the dark paths of the world are verily thought to be eternal; by the one (the bright path) a man goes not to return and by the other (the dark path) he returns.

anvaya.meaning: the bright and the dark — these two courses
of the world are deemed eternal
by one, one goes to non-return
by the other, one comes back again

verses.explanation: The two itineraries stamped as permanent fixtures of the cosmos: śukla and kṛṣṇa, the bright way and the dark, always open, always carrying their respective traffic. Beneath the old astronomy, a clean fork: knowledge exits, merit revolves. Every life is, knowingly or not, buying one of the two tickets.
verses.connection: Summarizes 8.24–25 as standing cosmic law — before the next verse tells Arjuna what to do about it.
verses.reflection: Both paths are eternal but not equal; choose, in one decision today, the one of light.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.

verses.devanagari:
नैते सृती पार्थ जानन्योगी मुह्यति कश्चन ।
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु योगयुक्तो भवार्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
naite sṛtī pārtha jānanyogī muhyati kaścana
tasmātsarveṣu kāleṣu yogayukto bhavārjuna
verses.essence: Knowing these two paths, Partha, no yogi is deluded. Therefore at all times, Arjuna, be steadfast in yoga.
verses.translation: Knowing these paths, O Arjuna, no Yogi is deluded; therefore at all times be steadfast in Yoga.

anvaya.meaning: knowing these two paths, O Partha
no yogi whatever is deluded
therefore, at all times
be yoked in yoga, O Arjuna

verses.explanation: The lore's practical cash-value, and it is deliberately deflating: knowing the schedule frees one from anxiety about the schedule. The yogi does not time his death to the bright fortnight; he makes himself bright at all times — sarveṣu kāleṣu, the chapter's refrain returning — so that any hour of departure is the right one.
verses.connection: Resolves the two-paths teaching the same way 8.7 resolved the final-thought law: constancy beats timing.
verses.reflection: Let knowledge of the two paths remove anxiety, not add it — the yogi knows, and is not bewildered.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.

verses.devanagari:
वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु चैव
दानेषु यत्पुण्यफलं प्रदिष्टम् ।
अत्येति तत्सर्वमिदं विदित्वा
योगी परं स्थानमुपैति चाद्यम्॥
verses.transliteration:
vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva dāneṣu yatpuṇyaphalaṃ pradiṣṭam
atyeti tatsarvamidaṃ viditvā yogī paraṃ sthānamupaiti cādyam
verses.essence: Whatever fruit of merit is assigned to the Vedas, sacrifices, austerities and gifts — the yogi who knows this passes beyond it all, and reaches the supreme primal abode.
verses.translation: Whatever fruit of merit is declared (in the scriptures) to accrue from (the study of) the Vedas, (the performance of) sacrifices, (the practice of) austerities, and gifts beyond all this goes the Yogi, having known this; and he attains to the Supreme Primeval (first or ancient) Abode.

anvaya.meaning: in the Vedas, sacrifices, and austerities
and in gifts, whatever merit-fruit is assigned
passes beyond all that, knowing this

anvaya.word: योगी परं स्थानम् उपैति च आद्यम्
anvaya.meaning: and the yogi attains the supreme, primal place

verses.explanation: The chapter's closing audit: tally every reward scripture ever promised — Vedic recitation, sacrifice, austerity, charity — and the knower of this teaching atyeti, overtakes the whole column. Not by despising those goods but by aiming past their ceiling: they purchase the revolving heavens of 8.16; this knowledge purchases the ādya sthāna, the first and final home.
verses.connection: Seals the chapter: against all of religion's negotiable rewards, one non-negotiable arrival.
verses.reflection: Aim past all listed merits today — study, rites, gifts, austerity — to what makes them small.
verses.background: Krishna closes with the two ancient paths of departure — the bright way of fire and light that does not return, and the smoke-dim way that does — and lifts the yogi who knows them beyond bewilderment, and beyond every merit of Vedas, sacrifice, austerity, and gift.
